Setting Up Google Analytics for Your Site

A friend nudged me to write this one. So here's how to embed Analytics (or GA — Google Analytics). If you've got a site or blog, I'd recommend setting up GA so you can actually see which posts are hits.
Sign up for Google Analytics first. After logging into your Google account, go to

The initial screen looks like this.
(I created a fresh account just for this post.)

In the form, pick the type that fits your case.
(Example uses Website.)
Fill in the fields. I'll use my
Account Name: any name you'll recognise
Website URL: watch for http vs https
Reporting Time Zone: auto-detected from your browser, can skip
You might get a terms-of-service popup — agree and submit.
snippet provided by Google Analytics — visible right after sign-up.
Usage is dead simple — copy the highlighted snippet and paste it into your page's
Pre-built blog systems usually have a field for the GA tracking ID. I'll use my own Blog as the example.
Open the GA tracking ID page and copy the tracking ID (Admin → Tracking Info → Tracking Code).
(around line 754). Paste the tracking ID into
Done. To verify, go back to the GA tracking code page and click
— wait a few seconds and you should see traffic show up.

Now the why. When you run a site, you'd love more readers and you probably write a bunch of posts — but without analytics you have no idea which posts are popular or worth promoting. That's where GA comes in.
For example, you can see the top pages visited in the last 28 days:
Adjust the date range at the top:
Say you want to look at visitors from 2019/04/29–2019/05/05 and break down by device. Adjust two things:
Data switches automatically — pretty convenient, right?

As a frontend dev, you might also want to see which browsers your visitors use.
(Audience → Technology → Browser & OS)
Want screen sizes instead? Click Screen Resolution at the top.

To wrap up — for a web dev, knowing GA is a plus. When you take on freelance work or refresh a company site, slap GA on for 1–2 weeks and you'll know which browsers and screen resolutions your visitors are on. Then you can optimise based on real data. Honestly a really handy tool.
GA can do a ton more, but the things I personally use it for are mostly screen resolution data and figuring out which posts are popular, so I can promote those further.
